threats.title: Genesis Depositors Steal Accumulated Gauge Rewards via Missing Reward Checkpoint
threats.hypothesis_description: depositsForGenesis calls updateReward(address(0)) instead of updateReward(tokenOwner), leaving userRewardPerTokenPaid[tokenOwner] at 0. When the genesis depositor later calls getReward(), earned() computes _balanceOf(user) * (rewardPerTokenStored - 0) / 1e18, granting retroactive rewards accumulated before their deposit. This directly steals rewards from prior stakers.
threats.investigation_rationale: The code path is unambiguous: updateReward(address(0)) updates global state but skips user-specific checkpoint. Any user deposited via genesis with pre-existing rewards in the gauge can claim those rewards. This is a standard reward accounting bug with direct fund extraction.

proof_of_signal.pattern: updateReward(address(0)) in _depositsForGenesis does not set userRewardPerTokenPaid[_tokenOwner] = rewardPerTokenStored

preconditions: notifyRewardAmount has been called at least once (rewardPerTokenStored > 0)
genesisPool calls depositsForGenesis for a user with no prior gauge interaction
User has userRewardPerTokenPaid == 0 (default)

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Genesis depositor calls getReward() after deposit, claiming rewards proportional to the entire rewardPerTokenStored history

economic_questions: What is the typical rewardPerTokenStored at time of genesis deposits?
How large are genesis deposit amounts relative to existing stakers?
Can this be exploited repeatedly across multiple gauges?
validation_requirements: Confirm updateReward(address(0)) path does not checkpoint user
Simulate genesis deposit after reward distribution and calculate claimable
Verify no other code path sets userRewardPerTokenPaid before getReward

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Up to entire accumulated reward pool at time of genesis deposit

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: Immediately after genesis deposit when rewardPerTokenStored is highest
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Drains reward tokens from gauge, reducing rewards for all legitimate stakers

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: rewardPerTokenStored value at genesis deposit time
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Compare earned() output with and without proper checkpoint

threats.title: Fee-on-Transfer Swap Routing Selects Wrong Pool for Amount Calculation Causing Systematic Failures
threats.hypothesis_description: _swapSupportingFeeOnTransferTokens calls RouterV2.getAmountOut() which returns the BEST output across both stable and volatile pools, but the actual swap executes on the pool specified by routes[i].stable. When the optimal pool type differs from the route's stable flag, the calculated output exceeds what the target pool can produce, causing K invariant failure and transaction revert. FoT token swaps become unreliable when both pool types exist.
threats.investigation_rationale: getAmountOut selects max(amountStable, amountVolatile) regardless of which pool the swap targets. The mismatch between quote source and execution target is a clear code defect causing either reverts or incorrect outputs.

proof_of_signal.pattern: getAmountOut returns best of stable/volatile but swap executes on routes[i].stable pool

preconditions: Both stable and volatile pairs exist for the same token pair
The optimal pool type differs from the route's stable flag
User is swapping fee-on-transfer tokens

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: amountOutput from getAmountOut uses best pool; swap executed on different pool
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Any FoT swap through the wrong pool type reverts, blocking legitimate trading
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Not directly profitable but causes systematic DoS on FoT swap functionality

economic_questions: How many token pairs have both stable and volatile pools?
What percentage of FoT swaps would be affected?
Can an attacker create the opposing pool type to break existing FoT swaps?
validation_requirements: Test FoT swap on pair where stable gives higher output but route specifies volatile
Verify K invariant failure on the target pool with inflated amountOutput

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Pair creation cost (~0.1 AVAX) to deploy opposing pool type
preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: DoS impact; indirect profit from broken swap routes
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Permanent once opposing pool type exists

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Small amount to seed opposing pool
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: FoT token swaps via this router become unreliable

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All FoT swap volume through affected router function

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compare stable vs volatile outputs for existing pairs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Trace execution path with mismatched pool types

threats.title: RouterV2.swapExactTokensForTokensSimple Is Non-Functional Due to Uninitialized Route Fields
threats.hypothesis_description: swapExactTokensForTokensSimple creates a route struct but never sets routes[0].pair or routes[0].receiver (defaulting to address(0)). For non-concentrated: getAmountsOut calls isPair(address(0))=false, returning amounts[1]=0, failing amountOutMin check. For concentrated: pairToDeployer(address(0)) returns invalid deployer. The function always reverts, wasting user gas. Users calling this core protocol function lose gas on every attempt.
threats.investigation_rationale: The route struct initialization clearly omits pair and receiver fields. This is a code defect that renders a primary swap entry point completely non-functional.

proof_of_signal.pattern: routes[0].pair and routes[0].receiver never set; default to address(0)

preconditions: User calls swapExactTokensForTokensSimple with any parameters

initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Function always reverts; if somehow bypassed, tokens sent to address(0)
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Gas waste for all users attempting to use this function

economic_questions: Is this function used by any frontend or integration?
How much gas has been wasted on failed calls?
Could edge cases allow partial execution sending tokens to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Call function with various parameters and verify consistent revert
Check if concentrated path could bypass address(0) check

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Aggregate gas costs; potential fund loss if somehow executed

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Trace all paths through the function
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify revert on both concentrated and non-concentrated paths

threats.title: RouterV2._swap Ignores _to Parameter - swapExactTokensForETH Fails When Route Receiver Misconfigured
threats.hypothesis_description: The _swap function completely ignores its _to parameter and uses routes[i].receiver for token destination. In swapExactTokensForETH, _swap is called with address(this) as _to, but if routes[last].receiver != address(this), WETH goes to the wrong address. The router then calls wETH.withdraw on tokens it doesn't have, reverting or losing funds. The 'to' parameter in public swap functions is dead code, violating the expected API contract.
threats.investigation_rationale: _swap iterates routes and sends output to routes[i].receiver, never referencing _to. For ETH unwrapping flows, this breaks the critical path where the router must receive WETH before converting to ETH.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _to parameter unused; routes[i].receiver controls all swap destinations

preconditions: User calls swapExactTokensForETH with routes where last receiver != router address
Or user calls swapExactTokensForTokens expecting 'to' parameter to control destination

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _swap never reads _to; only routes[i].receiver determines destination
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: WETH sent to wrong address; router's wETH.withdraw fails; user loses input tokens if tx partially succeeds
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: User fund loss due to API mismatch between documented behavior and actual code

economic_questions: Does the frontend always set routes[i].receiver correctly?
Can routes be constructed where receiver defaults cause fund loss?
How many existing integrations rely on the 'to' parameter?
validation_requirements: Call swapExactTokensForETH with mismatched receiver and verify behavior
Trace all swap paths to confirm _to is never used

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: User fund loss per misconfigured swap

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: API trust violation affecting all router integrators

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Users and integrators relying on 'to' parameter
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Full swap amount if tokens sent to wrong address
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All swap volume through affected functions

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Map all public swap functions to their _swap calls
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify receiver field propagation in all swap paths

threats.title: CustomPoolDeployer.createCustomPool Lacks Access Control Allowing Arbitrary Pool Creation with Manipulated Prices
threats.hypothesis_description: createCustomPool has no access control modifier (unlike setPlugin/setFee which use onlyAuthorized). Anyone can call it to create Algebra CL pools with arbitrary initialPrice values. Created pools are registered in algebraPoolAPIStorage as legitimate, potentially misleading routing algorithms and price oracles that query registered pools.
threats.investigation_rationale: Other privileged functions in the contract use onlyAuthorized, but createCustomPool does not. The function registers the pool in algebraPoolAPIStorage.setDeployerForPair(), making it appear as a valid protocol pool.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No onlyOwner or onlyAuthorized modifier on createCustomPool; other functions use onlyAuthorized

preconditions: entryPoint allows the deployer to create pools (likely by design)
Attacker can call createCustomPool with arbitrary parameters

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: initialPrice is attacker-controlled, sets arbitrary starting price
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Create pool with extreme price, registered as legitimate in algebraPoolAPIStorage
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Mislead routing to swap through manipulated-price pool

economic_questions: Does the entryPoint restrict pool creation beyond the deployer hook?
Do routing algorithms (BlackholePairAPIV2.getAmountOut) use registered pools for quoting?
Can attacker add minimal liquidity to manipulated pool to execute exploitative swaps?
validation_requirements: Call createCustomPool as non-authorized account and verify success
Check if BlackholePairAPIV2 routes through attacker-created pools

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Gas for pool creation (~0.1 AVAX) + minimal liquidity

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Minimal seed liquidity at manipulated price
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Could poison routing if pools are used for price discovery

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check entryPoint access control for deployer-initiated creation
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Test routing algorithm inclusion of attacker pools

threats.title: TradeHelper.getAmountInStable Returns Incorrect Values Due to Wrong Formula Direction and Decimal Conversion
threats.hypothesis_description: getAmountInStable has two math errors: (1) y_1 = reserveOut + output (should be reserveOut - output, since we remove output from the out-reserve), (2) amountInNoFees uses decimalsOut for conversion instead of decimalsIn. Both errors produce completely wrong amountIn values. GlobalRouter.getAmountInStable delegates to this function, so any on-chain integrator using it for swap sizing gets incorrect values.
threats.investigation_rationale: The formula direction error (+ instead of -) reverses the economic meaning of the calculation. Combined with wrong decimal conversion, returned values are meaningless. If used for on-chain trading decisions, this causes direct fund loss.

proof_of_signal.pattern: y_1 = reserveOut + output (line ~84, should be minus); amountInNoFees uses decimalsOut not decimalsIn (line ~107)

preconditions: Any caller invokes getAmountInStable for a stable pair
Caller uses returned value for trading decisions

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: y_1 = reserveOut + output reverses the direction of the Newton's method iteration
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Returns wrong amountIn; integrators overpay or underpay for trades
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Integrators using wrong quotes lose tokens on unfavorable swaps

economic_questions: Which on-chain contracts call GlobalRouter.getAmountInStable?
How far off are the returned values from correct values?
Could the wrong values cause systematic arbitrage opportunities?
validation_requirements: Compare getAmountInStable output with correct calculation for known pair
Check if any on-chain protocol uses this for swap execution

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Proportional to integrator swap volume using wrong quotes

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Incorrect pricing for all stable pair amountIn calculations

potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Varies; could be significant per trade
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All stable swap volume routed via incorrect quotes

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Compute actual vs expected values for sample pairs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Verify formula against correct stable swap math

threats.title: GaugeExtraRewarder Uses Token Balance Instead of Gauge TotalSupply for Reward Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: GaugeExtraRewarder._pendingReward and updatePool use IERC20(TOKEN).balanceOf(GAUGE) as lpSupply instead of gauge._totalSupply(). If tokens are sent directly to the gauge without calling deposit(), balanceOf > _totalSupply, diluting the accRewardPerShare calculation. Conversely, the mismatch could occur from genesis pool accounting discrepancies. This causes incorrect reward distribution.
threats.investigation_rationale: The mismatch between actual balance and accounting totalSupply means reward per share calculations use an incorrect denominator. Direct token transfers to the gauge dilute rewards for all stakers without the sender receiving any benefit.
preconditions: gaugeRewarder is set on the gauge
Attacker can send LP tokens directly to gauge without deposit()
Or genesis pool accounting creates balance vs totalSupply mismatch

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: accRewardPerShare = reward * ACC_TOKEN_PRECISION / balanceOf(GAUGE), where balanceOf can differ from totalSupply
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Send LP tokens directly to gauge to inflate denominator, diluting rewards
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing attack: dilute extra rewards for all stakers at cost of donated tokens

economic_questions: What is the typical difference between balanceOf(GAUGE) and totalSupply?
Can genesis deposits create persistent mismatches?
How significant is the reward dilution?
validation_requirements: Compare balanceOf(GAUGE) vs totalSupply in deployed gauges
Simulate direct token transfer and measure reward impact

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until tokens are recovered via emergency or other means

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Reduced extra rewards for all stakers

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All gauge stakers with extra rewarder
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Total extra reward pool over dilution period

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check existing gauge balanceOf vs totalSupply discrepancies
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Calculate reward dilution from various donation sizes

threats.title: Pair Mint and Burn Not Paused During Swap Pause Enabling Stale-Price Liquidity Manipulation
threats.hypothesis_description: Pair.swap checks PairFactory.isPaused() but mint and burn have no pause check. During a protocol pause (intended for emergencies), an attacker can add one-sided liquidity at stale reserves. When trading resumes, the attacker swaps against their own manipulated liquidity to extract value from other LPs at favorable rates.
threats.investigation_rationale: The asymmetry between swap (paused) and mint/burn (not paused) creates a window where liquidity can be repositioned while the market price has moved but reserves haven't updated, enabling value extraction on unpause.

proof_of_signal.pattern: swap checks isPaused; mint (line 182) and burn (line 199) have no pause check

preconditions: Protocol is paused due to emergency
Market price diverges from pool reserves during pause
Attacker has capital to add one-sided liquidity

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Mint uses stale reserves for LP calculation while market price has moved
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Add liquidity at stale price during pause, arbitrage on unpause
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = price divergence * manipulated liquidity share

economic_questions: How often has the protocol been paused historically?
What is the typical price divergence during pause periods?
How much capital is needed to meaningfully skew reserves?
validation_requirements: Confirm mint/burn work during pause
Calculate arbitrage profit from typical price divergence during emergency

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Proportional to pool size for meaningful impact
preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Pause duration + one block after unpause

market_conditions_analysis.liquidity_requirements: Significant capital relative to pool reserves
market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: During protocol pause with large price divergence

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Pool reserves proportional to price divergence

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Historical pause events and price movements
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Model arbitrage profitability under various scenarios

threats.title: Pair.claimStakingFees Permissionless With Potentially Unset StakingFeeHandler Burns Accumulated Fees
threats.hypothesis_description: claimStakingFees is callable by anyone and sends accumulated staking fees to PairFactory(factory).stakingFeeHandler(). If stakingFeeHandler is address(0) (never set or reset), tokens are transferred to address(0), permanently burning accumulated staking fees. PairFactory initializes stakingFeeHandler to address(0) and stakingNFTFee to 0, but if fees are enabled before handler is set, fees accumulate and can be burned.
threats.investigation_rationale: No access control on claimStakingFees, and no zero-address check on the recipient. The function reads handler from factory dynamically, creating a window between enabling staking fees and setting the handler address.
threats.config_evidence: PairFactory.initialize sets stakingNFTFee=0 and stakingFeeHandler=address(0). No deployment script sets handler before fees.

proof_of_signal.pattern: No access control; stakingFeeHandler read from factory; no zero-address check

preconditions: stakingNFTFee > 0 (fee manager has enabled staking fees)
stakingFeeHandler == address(0) (not yet set or reset)
Token allows transfers to address(0)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: PairFees.withdrawStakingFees sends tokens to address(0) if handler unset
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Anyone calls claimStakingFees to burn accumulated protocol fees
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Griefing: permanent loss of accumulated staking fees

economic_questions: Is stakingFeeHandler set before stakingNFTFee is enabled in production?
How much staking fee has accumulated across all pairs?
Which tokens revert on transfer to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Check production stakingFeeHandler setting
Test claimStakingFees with handler=address(0) for common tokens

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Permanent loss of accumulated protocol fees

potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: All accumulated staking fees across all pairs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check current stakingFeeHandler and stakingNFTFee values

threats.title: Unbounded Referral Fee Setting Allows Fee Manager to Divert 100% of Swap Fees or DoS All Swaps
threats.hypothesis_description: PairFactory.setReferralFee and setCustomReferralFee have no upper bound check. Setting MAX_REFERRAL_FEE > 10000 causes _referralFee = amount * maxRef / 10000 > amount in Pair._update0/_update1, causing `amount -= _referralFee` to underflow (revert in Solidity 0.8.13). This permanently DoSes all swaps on all pairs. Even values near 10000 divert nearly all fees from LPs.
threats.investigation_rationale: setFee has MAX_FEE cap but setReferralFee has none. The referral fee is computed as a percentage of the already-calculated swap fee. Values > 10000 cause arithmetic underflow in _update0/_update1, breaking all swaps.

proof_of_signal.pattern: setReferralFee has no upper bound validation; amount -= _referralFee underflows if fee > 10000

preconditions: feeManager calls setReferralFee with value > 10000
dibs address is set (non-zero)

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: _referralFee = amount * maxRef / 10000; if maxRef > 10000, _referralFee > amount, causing underflow
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Compromised feeManager sets referral fee to 10001+, permanently DoSing all swaps
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Complete protocol DoS; or at 9999, diverts 99.99% of fees from LPs

economic_questions: What is the current MAX_REFERRAL_FEE value?
Is feeManager a multisig or EOA?
What is the total daily swap fee volume at risk?
validation_requirements: Set referral fee to 10001 and verify swap revert
Calculate LP fee loss at various referral fee values near 10000

preliminary_attack_economics.potential_profit_range: Protocol-wide DoS or complete fee diversion
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Complete if fee diversion; destructive if DoS

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Total protocol shutdown or LP fee theft

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: All users (DoS) or all LPs (fee diversion)
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Complete loss of trading ability or fee income
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Entire protocol TVL and fee flow

threats.title: GaugeV2.setInternalBribe Tautological Validation Allows Zero Address Breaking Fee Distribution
threats.hypothesis_description: setInternalBribe has `require(_int >= address(0), 'ZA')` which always passes since all addresses are >= address(0) in uint160 comparison. This allows setting internal_bribe to address(0). When claimFees calls IERC20.approve(address(0), ...) and IBribe(address(0)).notifyRewardAmount(), the transactions revert, permanently blocking LP fee distribution to bribe system.
threats.investigation_rationale: The comparison `_int >= address(0)` is tautologically true for all address values. The intended check was likely `_int != address(0)` or `_int > address(0)`. Setting internal_bribe to address(0) breaks the fee forwarding flow.

proof_of_signal.pattern: require(_int >= address(0), 'ZA') is always true; allows address(0)

preconditions: Owner calls setInternalBribe(address(0)) (accidentally or maliciously)
claimFees is called on the gauge

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: address(0) >= address(0) is true; zero address accepted
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Setting internal_bribe=address(0) breaks claimFees with approve/call to zero
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: LP fees accumulate but cannot be distributed; permanent DoS on fee claiming

economic_questions: Can GaugeFactory.setInternalBribe also pass address(0)?
How much fee volume flows through claimFees?
Is there a recovery path once internal_bribe is set to address(0)?
validation_requirements: Call setInternalBribe(address(0)) and verify it succeeds
Call claimFees and verify it reverts

preliminary_attack_economics.attack_duration: Until owner fixes with correct address

market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: LP fee distribution blocked for affected gauge

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: LP fee recipients for affected gauge
potential_victim_impact.estimated_impact_per_user: Lost fee income during DoS period
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Accumulated LP fees in affected gauge

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Check current internal_bribe values across deployed gauges

threats.title: RouterV2 swapExactTokensForTokens Sends Initial Tokens to User-Controlled routes[0].pair Instead of Actual Pair
threats.hypothesis_description: In swapExactTokensForTokens, the initial token transfer uses routes[0].pair (user-controlled calldata) as destination, but _swap executes swaps via pairFor(routes[i].from, routes[i].to, routes[i].stable). If routes[0].pair differs from the actual pair, tokens are sent to the wrong address. While the attacker sends their own tokens, the _swap function may still succeed using any excess tokens already in the real pair (e.g., from direct transfers or FoT discrepancies), allowing the attacker to extract pair excess balances.
threats.investigation_rationale: The split between transfer destination (routes[0].pair) and swap execution target (pairFor) creates an inconsistency. If pairs hold any excess tokens beyond reserves, an attacker can extract them by sending their input to themselves while the swap uses the pair's excess.

proof_of_signal.pattern: _safeTransferFrom sends to routes[0].pair (user calldata) but swap uses pairFor() (computed)

preconditions: Target pair has excess token balance (balance > reserves)
Attacker crafts routes with routes[0].pair = attacker address
Excess balance sufficient for swap amountIn after K invariant check

initial_economic_evidence.suspicious_calculation: Transfer to routes[0].pair != pairFor(); swap on pairFor uses balance delta from existing excess
initial_economic_evidence.manipulation_vector: Attacker keeps input tokens while extracting pair excess via swap output
initial_economic_evidence.profit_hypothesis: Profit = extracted excess tokens - gas cost

economic_questions: Do pairs accumulate significant excess balances in production?
How often is skim() called to clear excess?
What is the typical excess balance across active pairs?
validation_requirements: Check balance vs reserves for deployed pairs
Simulate attack with routes[0].pair = attacker with pair having excess

preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_capital_needed: Minimal (only gas; input tokens are kept)
preliminary_attack_economics.estimated_profit_margin: Very high if excess exists (near-zero cost)

market_conditions_analysis.optimal_timing: After direct token transfers to pairs or FoT token swaps
market_conditions_analysis.market_impact_assessment: Drains excess tokens that would otherwise be skimmed or sync'd

potential_victim_impact.direct_victims: Anyone who sent excess tokens to pairs (donation, FoT discrepancy)
potential_victim_impact.total_value_at_risk: Aggregate excess balances across all pairs

investigation_priorities.parameter_analysis: Survey balance vs reserve discrepancies across deployed pairs
investigation_priorities.calculation_validation: Execute attack on test with pair holding excess tokens
